question_text: A radio station transmits a signal with a frequency of 100 MHz (100 × 10⁶ Hz). All electromagnetic waves travel at 3 × 10⁸ m/s. Calculate the wavelength of this radio signal. Show your working and explain why radio waves with such long wavelengths are better for broadcasting over long distances than visible light.
correct_answer: Wavelength = 3 metres. Radio waves have much longer wavelengths than visible light (which has wavelengths of 400-700 nm), and longer wavelengths can diffract around hills and buildings, allowing them to reach receivers over long distances.
explanation: Using the equation speed = frequency × wavelength: 3 × 10⁸ = (100 × 10⁶) × wavelength, so wavelength = 3 m. Long-wavelength waves diffract (bend) around obstacles better than short-wavelength waves, which is why radio broadcasts can reach areas that don't have a direct line of sight to the transmitter.
